facts: On 4 September 2025, hundreds of federal agents stormed a Hyundai electric vehicle battery plant in Georgia.
475 workers were arrested during the raid, 300 of them Korean nationals.
The event was the largest immigration raid at a single location in U.S. history.
The raid occurred at the Hyundai EV battery plant in Ellabell, Georgia.
The site covers 3000 acres.
The raid involved 500 federal, state, and local officers.
Most arrested workers were placed in federal detention in Folkston, Georgia.
Around 300 Koreans were placed on a special flight a week later and greeted by top government representatives.
Detainees described harsh conditions in U.S. detention, including eating near toilets, drinking foul-smelling tap water, and being crammed in cells with up to 70 people.
Many detainees had valid work visas.
A poll showed nearly 60 percent of South Koreans felt the raid was excessive.
South Korean president Lee Jae Myung ordered 'all-out efforts' in response and warned the action could 'jeopardize' Korean investment in the U.S.
The raid occurred just days after Lee met with U.S. President Donald Trump to discuss lucrative Korean investments.
According to the Council on Foreign Relations, the incident threatened $350 billion in planned Korean spending.
Foreign Minister Cho Hyun traveled to Washington for urgent talks.
On September 9, crowds protested outside the American Embassy in Seoul.
Construction of Hyundai’s 'Metaplant' was pushed back by several months due to the raid.
The plant is expected to create over 40,000 jobs, including at suppliers.
Georgia is America’s number one state for EV assembly and battery plant investment.

Special agent Steven Schrank described the raid as a 'criminal investigation into allegations of unlawful employment practices and serious federal crimes' at the Hyundai Motor-LG Energy Solution plant.
Federal officials initially claimed arrested workers were barred from working in the U.S. after overstaying visas or crossing illegally.
The warrant started for four Hispanic workers but escalated into a major showdown.
Most of the 300 Korean detainees worked for the battery plant, due to open in 2026.
Others were contractors at the Metaplant site.
Incentives, lower wages, and a union-free environment were central to Hyundai’s decision to locate in Georgia.
Hyundai accepted $1.8 billion in state incentives to build the Metaplant.
Groundbreaking for the Metaplant occurred in October 2022.
At the time of the raid, HMGMA and its onsite affiliates had created 2824 jobs.
Of 1364 positions at Hyundai, local residents held 73 percent, with only 49 expats directly employed.
Once complete, about 99 percent of the 8000 workers would be Americans.
The foreign-owned auto sector accounted for half of North American production by the time of the raid.
In 2023, the International Trade Administration documented this sector provided over 500,000 U.S. jobs with a total investment of $195.6 billion.
Most 'international automakers' were non-union, given free rein to hire, fire, and subcontract.
By 2025, the South was home to 13 automotive assembly plants.
In 2025, of all southern transplants, just one – a Volkswagen plant in Chattanooga, Tennessee – was unionized.
It took three elections (2014, 2019, and 2024) to secure the VW union breakthrough.
Between 1990 and 2007, the Big Three closed 29 North American factories.
Over the same period, foreign automakers largely from Japan and Korea opened 24 plants on the continent, mostly in the U.S. South.
From 1990 to 2006, total domestic vehicle production rose from 12.5 to 16 million a year.
The South had become 'America’s new auto manufacturing belt' with the lowest levels of union density in the country.
Labor Notes declared Hyundai has a terrible safety record, history of evading unions, racial discrimination, and employing children at factories/suppliers.
The United Auto Workers (UAW) cited deeper labor issues and noted militarized federal crackdowns hurt safety at Hyundai.
The Guardian noted three workers had died in 2 years at the Metaplant.
Both Hyundai and Kia established a large assembly plant in West Point, Georgia, in 2006.
Both firms had a history of labor problems including strained relations with Korean unions, global union avoidance, and allegations of using child/prison labor at suppliers.
Both firms argued subcontractors were not their employees to elide responsibility.
Stephen J. Silvia argued foreign manufacturers in the 1980s/1990s perfected a 'union-avoidance playbook.'
Tactics included captive audience meetings, calling unions third parties only interested in dues, and increasing compensation before union votes.
Placing plants in small towns with scattered workforces eroded solidarity.
Companies shared information, law firms, and managers to gain enormous control.
According to the UAW, Hyundai cut corners on safety precautions, refused to respect workers' right to a union, and relied on immigrant labor exploitation.
The three Metaplant deaths epitomized deeper problems.
Greg LeRoy, Good Jobs First’s director, summarized that southern states pushed for Taft-Hartley and became right-to-work states after 1947 to suppress wages.
Between 1985 and 2016, union density in OECD nations fell from an average of 30 percent to 17 percent.
In the U.S., only about 10 percent of workers belonged to unions in 2025, down from over a third in the 1950s.
The UAW’s membership tumbled from 1.5 million in the late 1970s to 400,000 in 2019.
Pioneering Honda, Nissan, and Toyota factories established in the 1980s set the path for a non-union car industry in the lower Midwest or South.
Honda’s plant in Marysville, Ohio, began making cars in November 1982.
Toyota’s non-union factory in Georgetown, Kentucky, was announced that same month.
By 1990, the transplants had the capacity to make 2 million vehicles a year.
In October 1977, Honda announced its factory in Marysville initially to make motorcycles.
In 1937, General Motors was the first large U.S. corporation to sign a contract with a union following sit-down strikes.
Big Three contracts led to annual cost-of-living increases (1948) and fully company-funded pensions (1949).
In 1970, U.S. autoworkers earned the right to retire with a full pension after 30 years of service.
UAW president Douglas Fraser wrote to Honda president Kiyoshi Kawashima in 1982 that the UAW represented virtually 100% of all car assembly workers in North America.
Fraser anticipated the union would organize Japanese automakers building in the U.S.
In the early 1980s, Honda initially pledged neutrality in Ohio but then fought the union.
Honda offered 'associates' benefits like subsidized meals and on-site leisure facilities to encourage company identification.
By 1993, UAW regional director Jack Sizemore reported internal difficulties organizing in largely non-union areas surrounding Honda plants.
An Automotive News retrospective called the non-union Marysville plant one of the industry’s 'watershed moments.'
Corporate analyst Karl Brauer admitted future costs of a unionized plant will almost assuredly be higher due to dues, payments, and benefits.
From 1980 to 1992, Republicans held the presidency, pushing a pro-business agenda that hurt unions.
Bieber cited the Reagan administration’s hostility to workers and sharp declines in union density.
He complained privately that Reagan and Bush administrations created a 'free-for-all economic environment' generating job losses and weakened contracts.
A Nissan factory in Smyrna, Tennessee (established 1980) upped the ante on union avoidance.
Plant manager Marvin Runyon dismissed the UAW as an unwanted 'third party.'
In 1989, Nissan easily beat the UAW in an NLRB election.
The Smyrna plant set a manufacturing blueprint favoring lower costs, business-friendly policies, and non-union labor.
Early factories hired hundreds of Japanese nationals, housing them in temporary on-site units due to rural locations and skilled worker shortages.
In 1985, 158 Japanese citizens were employed at the Marysville plant.
At Toyota in Kentucky, the young factory was overseen by hundreds of Japanese engineers headed by plant manager Fujio Cho.
Trump posted on Social Media that while immigration laws were important, foreign investments were 'welcome.'
Trump stated he would 'look at the whole situation' and expressed hope that foreign employees would help American workers.
Trump promised those arrested could stay in the U.S. if they trained Americans.
All but one of the 300 Koreans refused the deal to train Americans.
The Atlanta Journal-Constitution reported the action had 'strained international relations, exposed visa loopholes and cast a shadow over Georgia’s prized battery plant deal.'
The American Immigration Council referenced Trump’s anti-immigrant policies and condemned the action as discriminatory, haphazard, and economically damaging.
Industry website InsideEVs called the backlash a 'gigantic failure' for Trump's tough-on-immigration law enforcement policies.
Reports stressed record funding for U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) and increased latitude to conduct raids.
In the first 6 months of 2025, the U.S. lost over 1.2 million immigrants due to these policies.
CNBC concluded the raid raised issues from an 'immigration enforcement standpoint.'
Jeff Wasden from State Business Executives called on the White House to focus on the workforce and fix program problems.
Automotive News editorialized against the raid, warning that companies should be able to count on the U.S. as a safe place to invest and work.
The swoop highlighted the industry’s 'visa problem,' with automakers forced to use an 'ill-fitting' system to access vital foreign staff.
A 2023 Good Jobs First study 'Power Outrage' questioned EV subsidies, finding factories created 'substandard jobs.'
The report recommended companies pay 'market-level' wages and disclose labor/safety violations.
Key moves by predecessor companies like Honda, Mercedes-Benz, and Nissan showed escaping organized labor was integral to the sector's history.
source_title: Full article: A big deal: the Hyundai battery plant raid and the deeper labor history of the foreign-owned auto sector in the U.S.

facts: Hyundai Motor Group committed over $20 billion between 2021 and 2024 to establish a vertically integrated EV and battery manufacturing ecosystem in the United States.
Hyundai announced landmark joint venture partnerships with LG Energy Solution and SK On to build massive battery production hubs in Georgia.
Hyundai formed early-stage technology partnerships with Factorial Energy for solid-state batteries and IonQ for quantum computing-based battery simulation.
The company launched “Hyundai Home” in late 2021 to expand into the consumer energy ecosystem, complemented by pilot projects testing second-life EV batteries for grid storage with OCI Solar Power.
Operations at Hyundai Motor Group Metaplant America (HMGMA) began in April 2025.
Hyundai committed $816 million to a new battery R&D hub in Anseong, South Korea.
HD Hyundai Electric secured a $100.5 million commercial contract for a 200 MWh Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) in Texas.
Hyundai announced the rollout of Vehicle-to-Home (V2H) and Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) services.
Hyundai partnered with Fermi America to develop hybrid nuclear-BESS solutions for AI data centers.

In November 2025, Hyundai and Kia committed 1.2 trillion won ($816 million) to build their first dedicated battery R&D campus in Anseong, South Korea, focusing on next-generation cells and internalizing core technology.
In November 2025, Hyundai announced a domestic investment plan of 125.2 trillion won (~$85.7 billion) for 2026-2030 targeting future mobility, EV battery technology, and hydrogen.
In October 2025, Hyundai committed nearly $5.1 billion in India, including a flexible battery plant for locally assembled packs to support 250,000 EV units by 2030.

Between September 2023 and September 2025, Hyundai confirmed a total investment of $12.6 billion in Georgia for HMGMA and associated battery JVs with LGES and SK On to create a vertically integrated EV production hub with a capacity of 500,000 vehicles.
In March 2025, Hyundai announced a broad $21 billion investment in the United States from 2025 to 2028 to advance electric mobility leadership, including EV and battery manufacturing facilities.
In March 2024, Hyundai announced a global investment of 68 trillion won (~$50 billion) through 2026, with 35.5 trillion won (~$26 billion) allocated for R&D on EV infrastructure and manufacturing.
In October 2023, HD Hyundai’s subsidiary invested €45 million in European solid oxide fuel cell manufacturer Elcogen for green hydrogen production and marine propulsion systems.
In July 2021, Hyundai Motor Group and LGES jointly invested $1.1 billion to build a 10 GWh battery cell plant in Karawang, Indonesia.
In December 2025, Hyundai renewed and expanded a partnership with Air Liquide to accelerate the global hydrogen ecosystem for heavy-duty mobility in Europe, the U.S., and South Korea.
In October 2025, HD Hyundai Electric signed an MoU with Hitachi Energy to explore joint High-Voltage Direct Current (HVDC) projects in South Korea.
In September 2025, Hyundai partnered with Korea Southern Power, Alpha Asset Management, and KBI Group for a $100.5 million EPC contract for the Rutile BESS project in Texas.
In August 2025, Hyundai and Kia agreed to enhance collaboration with Korean battery manufacturers like LGES to improve EV safety and performance.
In July 2025, Hyundai E&C partnered with Fermi America to co-develop a nuclear-based hybrid energy project in Texas for AI data centers.
In March 2025, Hyundai expanded its partnership with IonQ to leverage quantum computing for simulating battery chemistry.
In March 2025, Hyundai Engineering and UGT Renewables partnered with the Serbian government to develop a 1 GW solar power project integrated with a BESS.
In March 2025, HD Hyundai collaborated with TerraPower to scale the supply chain for Natrium next-generation nuclear reactors.
In February 2025, Hyundai announced a partnership with Samsung SDI to develop high-performance batteries for robotics applications.

In February 2025, Hyundai committed over $4.3 billion to establish the HL-GA Battery Company JV in Georgia with LGES to manufacture next-generation batteries for Hyundai, Kia, and Genesis EVs in North America.
In October 2024, Hyundai named Lithion Technologies its official partner in Canada for EV battery collection and recycling to recover up to 95% of components.
In September 2024, Hyundai signed an MOU with General Motors to explore a global alliance on EVs and clean energy technologies.
In August 2024, Hyundai Glovis partnered with Huayou Cobalt to establish a global recycling system for EV batteries.
In April 2024, Hyundai entered a Joint Development Agreement with SES AI to build one of the world’s largest Lithium-Metal battery lines.
In April 2024, Hyundai signed a strategic partnership with CATL to secure a stable supply of advanced batteries for Beijing Hyundai EV projects.
In April 2024, Hyundai partnered with Exide Energy Solutions to localize EV battery production in India focusing on LFP cells.
In April 2023, Hyundai established a $5 billion JV with SK On to build an EV battery cell plant in Bartow County, Georgia, with an annual capacity of 35 GWh for 300,000 EVs.
In November 2021, Hyundai partnered with Factorial Energy to jointly test and develop solid-state battery technology for future Hyundai EVs.
Georgia became the epicenter of Hyundai’s strategy, with over $12.6 billion committed to HMGMA and massive battery JVs, driven by rising EV demand and U.S. manufacturing incentives.
Hyundai secured a foothold in Southeast Asia via the $1.1 billion Indonesia plant and initiated its India strategy through the Exide Energy partnership for LFP localization.
From 2025 onwards, Hyundai broadened its geographic focus to Europe with a 1 GW solar-plus-storage project in Serbia and a planned commercial V2G launch in the Netherlands.
The company is doubling down on South Korea with the $816 million Anseong R&D hub and $85.7 billion domestic investment plan for 2026-2030.
During 2021–2024, Hyundai scaled conventional lithium-ion manufacturing through LGES and SK On JVs while piloting second-life batteries with OCI Solar Power and CPS Energy in Texas.
Next-generation technologies including solid-state (Factorial), Lithium-Metal (SES AI), and quantum simulation (IonQ) were in deep R&D during 2021–2024.
In 2025, Georgia battery JVs transitioned from construction to full commercial operation.
Stationary energy storage moved from pilot to commercial-scale with the $100.5M Texas BESS contract.
V2G technology advanced from concept to a large-scale pilot in the Netherlands with an imminent commercial launch.

Hyundai’s collaboration with Factorial yielded a U.S. patent for using copper in solid-state cells and established a pilot production line at its research center, with vehicle trials expected by 2027-2028.
The strategy shifted from financial planning to tangible operational assets, validated by the Texas BESS contract expanding Hyundai beyond automotive into broader energy provision.
Execution risks materialized when an immigration raid delayed the Georgia battery site, highlighting supply chain and construction vulnerabilities.
In-house battery production remains a 2027 target, maintaining medium-term reliance on partners despite the new Anseong R&D hub.
Hyundai’s opportunity scope expanded from consumer ecosystems to large-scale industrial and grid-level energy solutions.
Abstract execution risks became concrete events impacting timelines, with potential GM collaboration underscoring competitive pressure in the global EV/battery race.
The year ahead focuses on ramping up Georgia manufacturing assets (HMGMA, LGES, SK On), where delays or successes will directly impact market position and financial returns.
Successful completion of the $100.5M Texas BESS project by 2027 will establish HD Hyundai Electric’s credibility as a global EPC contractor.
Milestones on solid-state batteries from the Anseong R&D hub or Factorial will indicate Hyundai’s long-term competitive edge.
The success of the European V2G pilot in the Netherlands will determine the speed of global rollout and unlock recurring grid service revenue.

Hyundai is transforming from an automaker into an integrated energy powerhouse, expanding into utility-scale BESS, home energy solutions, and grid services alongside nuclear and hydrogen partnerships.
The company pursues a dual strategy: relying on LGES/SK On JVs for current mass production while aggressively internalizing core tech via the $816M R&D campus and next-gen battery partnerships.
HD Hyundai Electric’s Texas BESS marks its entry into the utility energy market, with commercial V2G rollout starting in Europe.
The Fermi America partnership demonstrates expansion into industrial-scale integrated energy systems for AI data centers.

Biggest challenges include execution risk from capital-intensive projects (evidenced by the Georgia raid) and medium-term reliance on JV partners for core manufacturing, which creates potential bottlenecks.
Hyundai is mitigating partner reliance by building in-house R&D capabilities, though medium-term success remains tied to partner performance and managing complex global projects on time and budget.
source_title: Hyundai’s 2025 Battery Strategy: An Energy Powerhouse - EnkiAI

facts: Early in the Biden administration, an EV bubble reached its apex with Southern Republican governors seeking to establish EV plants and supplier networks in Dixie.
Under Governor Brian Kemp (R), Georgia became ground zero for the EV hype, with Kemp described as more committed to the EV agenda than any other governor.
The Biden administration steered significant government money to EV-adjacent promoters, shifting company focus from revenue to securing grants, incentives, and loans.
Aspen Aerogels announced plans in early 2022 to build a massive plant near Savannah that would employ 250 people and produce thermal barriers to suppress EV fires.
Construction on the Aspen Aerogels plant was cancelled after the company ceased work on the partially built factory, jeopardizing the promised 250 jobs.
Governor Kemp and Georgia leaders provided taxpayer dollars to incentivize Aspen Aerogels to build the plant for an industry that did not yet exist.
Aspen Aerogels never turned a profit over four full years, accumulating losses of $187 million, with its stock down 85% from its 2021 peak.
Aspen Aerogels executives announced during an investor call that construction on the 500,000-square-foot factory near Register (about 12 miles outside Statesboro) is indefinitely halted.

The Aspen Aerogels factory broke ground and received approval for a $671 million federal loan last fall as part of a flurry of clean tech financing approved in the waning months of the Biden administration.
Freyr Battery scrapped its multi-billion dollar plant plans that were intended to employ 700 Georgians.
In a 2024 interview, Freyr Battery's CEO stated the company’s purpose is to decarbonize transportation and storage systems by making sustainable batteries.
The planned Freyr factory would have built batteries to store renewable electricity and release it later, and would have been the second-largest battery factory worldwide when announced in 2023.
Freyr Battery, a startup founded in 2018, never began construction on its 368-acre site.
The Georgia Department of Economic Development conveyed a $7 million grant to Freyr Battery to purchase a site in Newnan, about 35 miles southwest of Atlanta.
Department spokesperson Jessica Atwell stated the state and company are working together to ensure the grant money is repaid expeditiously.
Freyr Battery lost $83 million through the first three quarters of its current fiscal year and combined losses of $173 million over the previous two full years.
Freyr Battery's stock price collapsed by 85% since its peak in 2022.
Rivian's Georgia plant, which was supposed to begin building electric vehicles by 2024, remains an empty field.

Rivian continues to receive financial support from the Biden administration and Volkswagen but has no path to profitability, reporting a $4.7 billion loss for fiscal year end 2024 compared to a $5.4 billion loss the prior year.
Rivian's annual sales have plateaued at approximately 50,000 units, aided by Biden-era tax incentives that are likely ending soon.
Rivian's existing plant in Normal, IL has a capacity of over 200,000 units per year, making it impossible for the empty Georgia field to produce the promised 400,000 vehicles per year.
Governor Kemp provided Rivian with over a billion dollars in incentives back in 2021.
Rivian's stock is down 92% from its peak.
Novonix, located in Tennessee, received significant hype and taxpayer money as a promised graphite supplier for the EV manufacturing industry.
Novonix's website states it is developing innovative technologies and materials for the EV and energy storage industries, with full production now targeted for 2028.
Novonix claims to have a proprietary synthetic graphite process currently scaling commercial production while simultaneously requesting property tax incentives and announcing major layoffs.
Novonix leaders asked the Chattanooga City Council to support a $54 million net tax incentive deal while laying off about a fifth of its Chattanooga-area employees.

Novonix lost $28 million through the first two quarters of its current fiscal year after losing $46 million in the previous full year, with its stock collapsing 94% since peaking after its 2022 public offering.
Novonix's CPA warned that financial conditions give rise to material uncertainty casting significant doubt over the company's ability to continue as a going concern.
source_title: THE MORNING RANT: Taxpayer-Subsidized “Clean Tech” Ventures in Support of the “EV Transition” Are Starting to Fold

facts: Algoma Steel Group Inc. is a leading Canadian producer of hot and cold rolled steel sheet and plate products.
The company supplies steel products to customers in North America.
Its products serve applications in the automotive, construction, energy, defense, and manufacturing sectors.
Algoma operates a state-of-the-art Direct Strip Production Complex (DSPC), which is one of the lowest-cost producers of hot rolled sheet steel (HRC) in North America.
The company is modernizing its plate mill and adopting electric arc technology to significantly lower carbon emissions as part of a transformation toward becoming a leading producer of green steel.
Algoma Steel Group Inc. will release its 2025 first quarter financial results after the market closes on Tuesday, April 29, 2025.
A webcast and conference call to review Q1 2025 results will be held on Wednesday, April 30, 2025, at 11:00 a.m. Eastern Time.
Michael Moraca serves as Vice President – Corporate Development and Treasurer for Algoma Steel Inc.
Honda Motor Co., Ltd. filed consolidated interim financial statements for the fiscal third quarter ended December 31, 2021 with the Financial Services Agency in Japan.
Travel restriction measures affected production activities at some of Honda’s production bases due to employee commute restrictions and delays in parts supply within the supply chain.

Some dealers were obliged to suspend business, shorten business hours, or reduce services such as inspections and repairs, but there was no significant impact on consolidated financial results for the nine months ended December 31, 2021.
Honda’s consolidated sales revenue for the nine months ended December 31, 2021 increased by 11.8%, to ¥10,677.0 billion from the same period last year.
Operating profit for the nine months ended December 31, 2021 increased by 50.3%, to ¥671.6 billion from the same period last year.
Profit before income taxes for the nine months ended December 31, 2021 increased by 28.3%, to ¥845.2 billion from the same period last year.
Profit for the period attributable to owners of the parent for the nine months ended December 31, 2021 increased by 31.1%, to ¥582.1 billion from the same period last year.
Sales revenue from external customers in the Motorcycle Business for the nine months ended December 31, 2021 increased by 27.3%, to ¥1,602.0 billion from the same period last year.
Operating profit in the Motorcycle Business for the nine months ended December 31, 2021 increased by 52.5%, to ¥232.3 billion from the same period last year.
Honda Group Unit Sales for the Automobile Business for the nine months ended December 31, 2021 were 3,000 thousand units, a decrease of 12.4% from the previous year.
Consolidated Unit Sales for the Automobile Business for the nine months ended December 31, 2021 were 1,792 thousand units, a decrease of 5.6% from the previous year.
Sales revenue from external customers in the Automobile Business for the nine months ended December 31, 2021 increased by 7.4%, to ¥6,668.6 billion from the same period last year.
Operating profit in the Automobile Business for the nine months ended December 31, 2021 increased by 258.3%, to ¥188.5 billion from the same period last year.
Sales revenue from external customers in the Financial Services Business for the nine months ended December 31, 2021 increased by 14.1%, to ¥2,114.3 billion from the same period last year.
Operating profit in the Financial Services Business for the nine months ended December 31, 2021 increased by 3.0%, to ¥258.1 billion from the same period last year.
Sales revenue from external customers in the Life Creation and Other Businesses for the nine months ended December 31, 2021 increased by 28.5%, to ¥291.9 billion from the same period last year.
American Honda Motor Co., Inc. integrated certain functions of Honda R&D Americas, Inc. for the three months ended June 30, 2021.

Honda of America Mfg., Inc. merged with Honda Manufacturing of Alabama, LLC, Honda Manufacturing of Indiana, LLC, Honda Transmission Mfg. of America, Inc., Honda R&D Americas, Inc. and three other companies and changed its name to “Honda Development and Manufacturing of America, LLC” for the three months ended June 30, 2021.
Honda of the U.K. Manufacturing Ltd. and Honda Turkiye A.S. ceased production of automobiles for the three months ended September 30, 2021.
American Honda Motor Co., Inc. made organizational changes to its automobile manufacturing-related companies and automobile development function in the United States on April 1, 2021.
As a result of U.S. organizational changes, Honda R&D Americas, Inc. and Honda Engineering North America, Inc. were merged into Honda Development and Manufacturing of America, LLC.
In motorcycle operations, Honda of South Carolina Mfg., Inc. and the PSPs development function of Honda R&D Americas, Inc. were merged into the PSPs division of American Honda Motor Co., Inc.
Consolidated cash and cash equivalents on December 31, 2021 decreased by ¥67.5 billion from March 31, 2021, to ¥2,690.4 billion.
Net cash provided by operating activities for the nine months ended December 31, 2021 amounted to ¥947.8 billion of cash inflows.
Net cash used in investing activities for the nine months ended December 31, 2021 amounted to ¥381.7 billion of cash outflows.
Net cash used in financing activities for the nine months ended December 31, 2021 amounted to ¥692.1 billion of cash outflows.

Headquartered at 150 Interstate North Parkway, Atlanta, Georgia 30339, U.S.A.
Public company incorporated in 1930 with 4,100 employees and $1.8 billion in sales (2005).
Stock trades on NASDAQ under ticker symbol SPSX.
Classified under NAIC code 331528 Other Nonferrous Foundries.
Leading manufacturer of cable and wire among the largest industry players in North America with a significant global presence.
Operates 23 production facilities in the United States, Mexico, and Europe with an established global distribution network.
Customer base includes cable television and telephone companies, as well as distributors and manufacturers in a variety of industries.
Offers more than 500 magnet wire and winding wire products marketed through Essex Group Inc. in North America and China.
Serves the European market through its EssexNexans joint venture.
Magnet wire products are critical components in millions of items, including automobiles, home appliances, generators, motors, transformers, and commercial machinery.
World's largest producer of outside plant copper communications cables via Superior Essex Communications LP.
Serves original equipment manufacturers and the motor repair market via its Essex Brownell, Essex Express, and Active Industries units, which offer various electrical insulation products.
Formed in 1998 from the combination of Essex Group Inc. and Superior TeleCom.
Deepest roots trace to Detroit, Michigan, where Addison E. Holton established Essex Wire Corp. in 1930.
During its formative years, produced battery cables, wiring harnesses, and other wire items for the Ford Model A from leased space in Ford Motor Company's Highland Park plant.
Acquired Logansport, Indiana-based RBM Manufacturing Company, a producer of electrical switches and automotive components, during the 1930s.
During the 1940s produced enough magnet wire to build millions of transformers used by America and its allies during World War II.
Essex plants also produced thousands of miles of field telephone wire for the Army Signal Corps, as well as wiring harnesses for B-24 bombers.
Awarded the highly prestigious 'E' Award by the Secretary of War.
Relocated headquarters to Fort Wayne, Indiana in 1954.
Walter F. Probst succeeded Addison Holton as president in 1958; Holton retired in 1962, at which time Probst assumed board chairman.
By 1965 sales totaled $355 million with seven divisions, 41 plants throughout North America, and 26 warehouses.
First listed on the New York Stock Exchange in 1965 with 5 million shares.

Unveiled an $8 million expansion initiative in 1965, including $3.2 million for its Essex Wire and Cable division, $2.5 million for its Essex Automotive division, and $2.3 million for the acquisition, expansion, and improvement of other facilities.
Paul W. O'Malley named company president in early 1966 after being with Essex since 1952; Probst continued as chairman and CEO.
By 1968 in the process of building a new 30,000-square-foot facility in Fort Wayne for its Metal Products division, bringing total plants to 67 in 30 states and Canada.
Acquired Transport Motor Express Inc., one of the nation's largest trucking companies, in a stock deal valued at $12 million in 1968.
Essex Wire Corporation operated under the name Essex International Inc. by 1970 with an employee base of 25,700.
Sales reached approximately $845 million in 1973 while operating about 100 plants.

Merged with East Hartford, Connecticut-based United Aircraft Corporation (United Technologies Corporation) in 1974, creating the nation's 30th largest company with combined 1973 sales of $3.13 billion.
Walter Probst retired as chairman and CEO in 1974, succeeded by Paul O'Malley; O'Malley was elected Essex group vice president and director until retiring in 1981.
James A. O'Connor, a 31-year Essex veteran, succeeded O'Malley in 1981.
During the late 1970s faced criticism from labor leaders for being one of the most anti-labor companies in the industrial north.
AFL-CIO criticized the company for encouraging multiple unions to keep labor decentralized and weak, scattering production to diffuse strikes, paying low wages, and providing poor benefits packages.
In September 1978 the U.S. Justice Department charged Essex and five other wire manufacturers with price fixing.
Company denied wrongdoing while the agency charged that Essex and its counterparts exchanged information about prices and conspired to restrain trade.
Founder Addison Holton died at age 83 in 1980.
In 1982 announced plans to invest $3 million in its Fort Wayne facility, which would become the company's world headquarters, scheduled for completion in 1984.
By 1983 employed 8,982 workers worldwide with an annual payroll of $26.8 million.
Became a privately owned firm in 1988 when MS/Essex Holdings bought the company from United Technologies; John M. Bruce was president and CEO at the time.
Purchased by Bessemer Holdings in 1992 but remained privately owned until becoming publicly traded again in 1997.
Acquired magnet wire and electrical motor distributor Brownell in 1995, merging it into Essex's distribution arm as Essex Brownell.
Acquired Active Industries, a converter/fabricator of electrical insulation products, in 1998.
In September 1998 announced being acquired by Superior Telecom Inc. in a deal valued at $1.4 billion in cash, stock, and debt.
Acquisition finalized in 1999, making Essex a subsidiary of Superior and creating North America's largest cable and wire manufacturer.
Essex Chairman, President, and CEO Steven Abbott became Superior's president and chief operating officer following the deal.
Superior Telecom started in 1954 as Superior Cable Corporation in Hickory, North Carolina, producing plastic insulated, twisted pair cable and wire.
Production took place in a new 32,000-square-foot plant authorized by Hickory Development Corp. at a cost of $160,000.
Manufacturing equipment installed in May, with first reel delivered to Hickory Telephone Company in August.
Attorney Walker Geitner was Superior's first president; named chairman in 1959.
James L. Robb succeeded Geitner as president after being with the firm since its inception.
Established a second manufacturing plant in Rocky Mount, North Carolina in 1960.
By 1962 expanded headquarters plant three times to 150,000 square feet, adding production for coaxial cables and wire for missile, aircraft, and industrial applications.
In 1963 played a pioneering role in the telephone sector with the roll-out of the industry's first all-plastic drop wire.
Erected a new cable plant in Brownwood, Texas in 1964 to serve the southwestern United States.

By 1965 home plant reached 125,000 square feet of manufacturing space, about 35,000 square feet of office space, and a 35,000-square-foot building for warehousing and shipping; annual sales reached nearly $20 million.
Plans included constructing a separate facility devoted to research and development.
Acquired by Continental Telephone in 1967 and renamed Superior Continental Corp.
Annual sales reached $90 million in 1969 and approximately $125 million in 1971 under new ownership.
Announced plans to build an eight-story corporate office facility in Hickory for $3 million in 1971.
J. L. Robb became president of manufacturing for Continental Telephone, and Warner T. Smith served as Superior Continental's president.
Hickory manufacturing plant grew to 600,000 square feet.
Divested by Continental Telephone in 1976; Robert C. Pittman and A. L. Viles purchased it for $17.1 million in a cash and stock deal.
Reincorporated as parent company of Superior Cable facilities in Hickory and Rocky Mount, North Carolina, plus Brownwood, Texas plant.
Deal included Hickory Brand Telephone Cords, Keller, Texas-based Communication Apparatus Company, and a non-operating plant in Angier, North Carolina.
Acquired by optical fiber and cable manufacturer Siecor Corporation in 1980; Siecor relocated from Horseheads, New York to Hickory in 1981.
Divested by Siecor five years later (1985) as an independent firm marketing its own line of fiber optic cable.
In 1987 Siecor convinced Japan to purchase some of its fiber optical cable after five years of negotiating.
Alpine Group Inc. acquired Superior in 1993, adding premises data cables to its product lineup and acquiring other firms.
Superior TeleCom Inc. went public in 1996 with a listing on the New York Stock Exchange; annual sales reached $500 million in 1997.
Held about 30 percent of the market for copper building wire by August 1999.
Announced plans to close three wire manufacturing plants and lay off 469 employees in order to optimize financial returns in a highly competitive market suffering from oversupply.

Closed plants in Glendale, Arizona; Pauline, Kansas; and Tiffin, Ohio while continuing production at sites in Sikeston, Missouri; Florence, Alabama; Anaheim, California; Lafayette, Indiana; and Columbia City, Indiana.

In 2000 awarded a three-year contract to supply cable and wire to Alltel Communications Products Inc., which served 8.5 million customers in 25 states with both wireless and wired communication services.
Deal involved Superior Essex supplying Alltel with all of its cable and wire.
Launched an e-commerce Web site in late 2000 to boost sales, allowing order entry, shipment tracking, estimated arrival times, and industry news feeds.
By December 2001 occupied a 228,000-square-foot warehousing and distribution facility in northeast Indiana previously occupied by defense giant Raytheon.
Maintained a workforce of 5,000 in 2003, giving employees better benefits without increasing out-of-pocket contributions despite national healthcare cost trends.
Streamlined benefit packages and renegotiated contracts with healthcare providers to achieve this.
Stephen M. Carter was CEO as of early 2004.
In April 2004 announced plans to acquire the North American outside plant communications cable and wire operations of Belden Inc. for approximately $95 million.
Purchase freed Belden from a market where it had not been able to turn a profit for several years.
Carter stated the acquisition would align capacity in the outside plant copper communications cable industry with demand, which declined 50 percent in North America since 2000.
Consolidated manufacturing facilities and reduced capacity but needed to look beyond internal solutions to serve customers efficiently.
Confident they could smoothly transition Belden's customer base, which last year generated approximately $200 million in revenues, without disruption.
In February 2006 announced revenues reached $1.8 billion in 2005, up from $1.4 billion the previous year.
Carter attributed increased revenues to consolidating acquisitions and pointed to increased earnings despite skyrocketing energy costs.
Principal subsidiaries include Superior Essex Communications LP and Essex Group, Inc.
Principal competitors include Alcatel, APA Cables and Networks Inc., and General Cable Corporation.
Trenton Pressing operates a facility located at 505 N. Industrial Boulevard, Trenton, GA 30752.
The company is actively hiring for Production Operator positions.
A Production Operator job posting was published on July 18, 2025, by Mandy Whitehead.
Career application and employee resources are accessible through the company's dedicated Employee Portal.
The company maintains a Supplier Quality Manual for its vendors.
The company enforces a Code of Conduct & Corporate Responsibility Policy.

facts: Truck-trailer manufacturers anticipated an industry rebound in 2021 after the COVID-driven disruptions of 2020.
The economy bounced back significantly, but pandemic problems persisted regarding keeping workers on the job due to government assistance programs and finding new employees for surging demand.
Lack of steady material supply and predictable costs dragged production for many manufacturers.
Trailer manufacturers had "uncomfortable" conversations with customers regarding delivery schedules and pricing in 2021.
Nearly 270,000 units on books marked a 27% increase from the prior year.
This was the tenth consecutive year that trailer output exceeded 200,000 units.
Survey totals cannot be directly compared with domestic surveys excluding Canadian and Mexican plants; total build is larger than Top 25 numbers reported.
Survey methodology involves contacting management at each company; rankings reflect production volume, not profitability or revenue.
Hyundai Translead of San Diego, Calif., produced 55,792 truck-trailers in 2021, up 61% from 2020.
Hyundai Translead's total includes 51,101 dry freight vans; 4,308 refrigerated van trailers; and 383 flatbed trailers.
Hyundai Translead also built 11,553 container chassis and 1,497 converter dollies, not counted in rankings.
Trailers are built at Hyundai Translead's plants in Mexico.
Hyun Jin Noh, senior business strategy specialist at Hyundai Translead, reported production numbers.
Hyundai Translead added capacity to take advantage of supply partners' hard work despite 2021 supply chain challenges.
Wabash shipped 44,045 new trailers in 2021, a 21% increase compared to the prior year.
Wabash's total includes 36,640 dry freight vans; 2,875 refrigerated van trailers; 2,550 flatbeds; and 1,980 tank trailers.
Wabash also built 16,560 truck bodies and shipped 1,329 converter dollies, not counted in rankings.
President and CEO Brent Yeagy stated on a Feb 2 conference call that order backlog grew to another record level during the fourth quarter.
Yeagy credited the organization with constructing the backlog to provide options in 2022 if supply chain and workforce conditions improve.
Wabash remains focused on bringing new products and technologies to market, building out adjacent revenue streams, and growing beyond the equipment cycle.
Great Dane Limited Partnership built 38,200 trailers in 2021, up 11% over the previous year.
EVP of Sales Chris Hammond noted that supply chain and labor challenges affected the ability to fill all customer orders, pushing some into 2022.
Great Dane is optimistic that supply chains will begin to correct later in 2022, allowing growth from 2021 totals.
Utility Trailer Manufacturing Co. produced a total of 42,379 trailers, up 25% from 2020.
Utility's production includes 21,343 refrigerated trailers, 19,162 dry van trailers, and 1,874 flatbed trailers.
Marketing manager Brett Olsen cited labor market and supply chain challenges, with absenteeism from COVID variants taxing an undermanned workforce.
Utility currently has six trailer manufacturing facilities across Marion, Va.; Clearfield, Utah; Piedras Negras, Mexico; Glade Springs, Va.; Paragould, Ark.; and Enterprise, Ala.
Vanguard National Trailer Corp. produced 14,945 trailers in 2021, a 24% increase from 2020.
Vanguard's production includes 11,018 dry freight vans at its Monon, Ind. headquarters and Trenton, Georgia plant.
Vanguard built 3,297 refrigerated van trailers at its Monon plant and West Coast plant in Moreno Valley, Calif.
Stoughton Trailers produced 15,000 units in 2021, a 36% increase from the prior year.
President and CEO Bob Wahlin stated Stoughton focused on rebuilding its workforce, ramping dry van production, and installing an entirely new production line.
Stoughton purchased property for a new chassis manufacturing plant in Waco, Texas.
Stoughton installed laser cutting equipment and robotic welders to improve throughput and reduce dependency on manual labor.
MANAC Inc of Saint-Georges, Quebec, built 7,250 trailers in 2021, up 4% from 2020.
President Charles Dutil noted backlog levels would support higher output in 2022, though component availability may hold back production.
EnTrans International LLC of Athens, Tenn., reported shipments of 6,939 truck-trailers in 2021, a 15% increase from the previous year.
Senior VP Jake Radish noted all business unit backlogs are up covering well into 2023 with significant growth across segments.
EnTrans shipments include tank trailers built by Heil Trailer International of Athens, Tennessee, and Polar Tank Trailer of Holdingford, Minnesota.
EnTrans International LLC is owned by American Industrial Partners, which also owns JARCO, SERVA, and Polar Service Centers.
Fontaine Trailer Co., an Alabama-based manufacturer, manufactured 4,745 trailers in 2021, up 65% from 2020.
Commercial Platform President Alan Briley noted flatbed demand is outpacing production ability and supply chain challenges are stabilizing but not improving.
Fontaine utilizes consignment inventory programs with some supply chain partners to maintain safety stock.
Dorsey Trailer and Pitts Trailers built 4,645 trailers in 2021, a 5% increase.
President JP Pierson suggested output could have nearly doubled without supply chain and labor challenges.
Pitts Trailers implemented wage increases of 20% or more and instituted surcharges in 2021 to cover exorbitant material costs and increased logistics charges.
MAC Trailer Enterprises of Alliance, Ohio, built 6,200 trailers in 2021, a 49% increase over 2020 production.
MAC acquired Portland, Ore.-based Beall brand tankers from Wabash National at the end of 2021.
Timpte Inc in David City, Neb., built 4,037 hopper trailers in 2021, an increase of 23% from 2020.
President Dale Jones cited supplier shortages and unprecedented cost increases as challenges alongside labor availability issues.
Premier Trailer Mfg Inc in Visalia, Calif., built 2,046 trailers in 2021, a 16% decrease over the prior year.
GM Michael McGinn noted anticipation of a 20% increase in production in 2022 with initial orders for three new models including container chassis.
XPO Logistics Trailer Manufacturing in Searcy, Ark., built 2,021 units in 2021, a 14% improvement from 2020.
East Manufacturing Co of Randolph, Ohio, built 2,604 mainly aluminum trailers in 2021, up 29% from 2020.
VP Chris Cooler cited supply chain disruptions and hiring enough factory workers as the greatest challenges to increasing production capacity.
Strick Trailers built 1,800 truck-trailers in 2021, a 10% decline from the prior year.
Marketing manager Ben Katz stated the biggest headwind is difficulty getting needed parts, with daily challenges in this area.
Kentucky Trailer of Louisville, Ky., built 1,968 trailers in 2021, a decrease of 26%.
COO Gary Parker noted a significant pause in orders during April-May as Moving and Storage customers paused to see how events would develop.
Felling Trailers Inc of Sauk Centre, Minn., built 1,960 trailers with a 10K or larger axle in 2021, a 9% increase over the previous year.
IPO Fruehauf de Mexico built 3,623 truck-trailers in 2021, a 97% increase over 2020.
USA Commercial Director Don Brown anticipated a positive 2022 based on industry activity and the addition of a new facility in New Bowling Green, KY.
Fruehauf de Mexico has built trailers at its plant 20 miles north of Mexico City for over 50 years, employing some 500 workers.
Trail King Industries in Mitchell, S.D., built 1,850 truck trailers in 2021, an increase of 6% from 2020.
CFO Gene Astolfi cited supply chain issues, extended lead times, skyrocketing material costs, and labor shortages as hampering factors for 2022.
Reitnouer Inc in Birdsboro, Pa., built 2,627 trailers in 2021, a 72% increase from 2020.
President Bud Reitnouer noted constraints from labor and supply issues forced shorter-term pricing strategies due to ridiculous market conditions.
Talbert Manufacturing in Rensselear, Ind., built 1,230 trailers in 2021, a slip of 3% from 2020.
VP Troy Geisler cited supply chain interruptions, unpredictable material spikes, and limited labor pool as challenges requiring fluid planning.
Talbert added a second manufacturing plant in Liberty, North Carolina in 2014.
Western Trailer in Boise, Idaho, built 1,087 trailers in 2021, a 14% decrease over the previous year's production.
Doepker Industries in Anaheim, Sask., built 1,001 truck-trailers in 2021, down 34% from the previous year.
VP Devin Leonard noted supply chain challenges impacted output but expects increased output in 2022 while ramping up production in a newest facility in Georgetown, Ontario.
Tremcar Inc built 830 tank trailers and 260 tank trucks in 2021, with total tanks breaking even with the prior year.
Director of marketing and communications Melanie Dufresne cited parts supplier delivery delays, fluctuation of metal prices, and employee absences due to COVID-19 as challenges.
source_title: Wait 'til this year! | Trailer Body Builders

facts: Many automakers have detailed plans to electrify large portions of their fleets over the next decade, with some announcing goals for fully electrified lineups in as little as five years.
A record number of almost 100 pure battery electric vehicles (BEVs) are set to debut by the end of 2024 if all goes according to plan.

Gabe Shenhar, associate director of Consumer Reports' Auto Test Center, states that more affordable models have the potential to sway a significant percentage of car-buyers toward EVs due to efficiency, performance, and lower ownership costs.
American Honda aims for all its sales to be zero-emissions, electrified vehicles by 2040, with a projected ramp-up of 40 percent of sales by 2030 and 80 percent by 2035.
Honda sells popular Honda- and Acura-branded cars, SUVs, and pickups to American consumers.

Honda has previously sold electrified models ranging from the Civic hybrid, CRZ, and Insight to more recent models like the Accord hybrid, CR-V hybrid, and Clarity hybrid, plug-in hybrid, and electric.
Honda signed a memorandum of understanding with General Motors in late 2020 allowing Honda to use GM's upcoming Ultium batteries.
GM states its Ultium technology will allow long-range electric travel among other benefits.
The Honda-GM accord also calls for mutual vehicle platform-sharing, allowing either automaker to use the basic building blocks of GM's EV architecture.
Honda announced it will have its first electric vehicles based on the e:Architecture platform by model year 2024.
The Honda Prologue is scheduled to launch in early 2024 in North America.
Automotive News reported in January that Honda plans to build a Honda EV in 2023 at a GM plant in Mexico.
Honda also plans to build an Acura EV in 2024 at the GM plant in Tennessee where GM plans to build the Cadillac Lyriq EV.
BMW entered the EV market early with its i3 electric city car, available at U.S. dealerships for the 2014 model year.
BMW has teased concept EVs including the i4, iX, and Vision.
BMW recently announced plans to introduce electric versions of the 5 Series and 7 Series sedans, and the X1 SUV.
BMW says it will bring roughly a dozen new EVs to market by 2025.
Mini and Rolls-Royce fall under the BMW umbrella, with a Mini Cooper electric model already available.
Rumors suggest an upcoming Rolls-Royce EV that could be called the Silent Shadow.
Ford says its passenger vehicles will be 100 percent emissions-free in Europe by 2030 but has not made the same commitment in the U.S.
Ford sells an electric SUV called the Mustang Mach-E and an electric version of its commercial Transit van.
Ford plans to introduce an electric version of its popular F-150 pickup, dubbed F-150 Lightning, for sale in spring 2022.
Ford's Lincoln luxury brand projects that half its global sales will be zero-emissions models by the middle of this decade and will electrify its entire portfolio by 2030.
GM includes the Buick, Cadillac, Chevrolet, and GMC brands and has a long history with EVs dating back to the late 1990s with the EV1.
GM previously produced the Chevrolet Volt plug-in hybrid and the all-electric Chevrolet Bolt, which is currently the most reliable EV according to Consumer Reports' reliability survey.
GM announced in January that it aspires to have an all-electric model lineup by 2035, with 40 percent of its models being pure EVs by the end of 2025.
GM stressed it will not be making hybrids or plug-in hybrids, considering them transitional technology.
GM recently introduced a redesigned Bolt and the Bolt EUV SUV variant.
The GMC Hummer EV off-road truck is scheduled to arrive early in 2022, followed by a Hummer SUV, an electric Silverado-based pickup, and a pair of Cadillac EVs: the Lyriq SUV and the Celestiq sedan.
Hyundai already has the Kona Electric and Ioniq Electric models on the market and recently unveiled the first of three planned models in its all-electric Ioniq sub-brand, the Ioniq 5 SUV.
Genesis, Hyundai's premium brand, is expected to unveil its own EVs soon, and Hyundai aims to sell 1 million pure electric vehicles worldwide by 2025.
Jaguar and Land Rover have been a subsidiary of Tata Motors since 2008, with the Jaguar I-Pace electric SUV already in production.

In early 2021, the company announced all Jaguar and Land Rover models will have an electric version by the end of the decade, including six new electric Land Rover SUVs over the next five years and an all-electric Jaguar lineup by 2025.

Hyundai Motor Group owns a minority stake in Kia, which plans to release EVs riding on the same E-GMP platform, a modular EV-specific substructure with floor-mounted batteries that underpins Ioniq-branded EVs.
Kia already has an electric version of its Niro and will have 11 EVs—seven dedicated electric models—in its lineup by 2026, starting with the EV6.
Kia wants to sell 1.6 million "eco-friendly vehicles" globally by the end of the decade.
Mazda does not yet offer any electric cars in the U.S. but recently started selling the MX-30 EV and plug-in hybrid models in other markets.

Mazda plans to introduce an EV-only platform within the next few years, with all its nameplates available with some level of electrification by 2030, though it has not specified the proportion of pure electric versus plug-in or mild hybrids.
Mercedes' electric push began in 2007 with an EV version of the Smart ForTwo city car, and the automaker plans to introduce 10 new EV models by 2022.
The U.S. market will see the EQS, an electric version of the S-Class sedan, in fall 2021, while other EV models like the EQA and EQB SUVs are on hold for the U.S. but expected to debut overseas.
The EQS will have a 435-mile range based on European test standards, which tend to be more optimistic than EPA numbers.
Mitsubishi has a limited presence in North America but has been partially owned by Nissan since 2016 and has access to significant technical resources through the Renault-Nissan-Mitsubishi Alliance.

The company announced plans to expand its electrified lineup to 50 percent of global sales by 2030, having previously introduced the short-lived i-MiEV electric hatchback around the same time as the Nissan Leaf and already selling plug-in hybrid models.
Over the past decade, Nissan has sold half a million Leaf EVs worldwide, with its next North America-bound electric model being the Ariya small SUV scheduled to go on sale late in 2021.
Nissan has a hybrid technology called e-Power that uses a gasoline engine to generate electricity for an electric motor-driven powertrain, akin to how the Chevrolet Volt operated.

Peyman Kargar, chairman of Infiniti (Nissan's luxury marque), said in mid-2020 that his division would add a performance-oriented hybrid and a pure BEV to Infiniti's lineup, though he did not specify when.
Stellantis formed in early 2021 when Fiat Chrysler Automobiles merged with Groupe PSA, encompassing Alfa Romeo, Chrysler, Dodge, Fiat, Jeep, Maserati, and Ram truck brands in North America.

Stellantis announced a major worldwide EV initiative in July 2021, declaring all its brands committed to offering fully electric vehicles, key to which is the introduction of four electric platforms with ranges from 300 to 500 miles.
Stellantis aims for 40 percent of its U.S. models sold to be low-emissions vehicles and expects battery costs to drop significantly over the next few years.
By 2024, Stellantis expects to have two battery technologies in place: a high-density option and a nickel cobalt-free version, with solid state batteries available by 2026.
Jeep promises a "zero emission vehicle in every segment" by 2025, Dodge pledges an electric muscle car (a Charger) in 2024, and Ram plans a full-sized electric truck by 2024.

Subaru announced in early 2021 that it would offer some form of electrification on all its models by mid-decade but did not specify the proportion of vehicles that would be EVs, plug-in hybrids, or hybrids.

Tesla launched with a roadster, followed by the Model S hatchback and Model X SUV, known for impressive driving range and technological innovation particularly in over-the-air updates and driver assistance systems.
The Model 3 sedan has broadened the brand's appeal, while the line includes the recent Model Y crossover delivering a driving range of 300-plus miles but a stiff ride.

The Model S recently received a significant update with a freshened exterior and interior as well as a new Plaid high-performance version, and Tesla expects to see a pickup truck and second-generation roadster in the near future.
Along with its luxury division Lexus, Toyota has built cars and SUVs with hybrid powertrains for decades, selling more than 6 million Prius hybrids in 20 years of production.

Toyota moved into hydrogen fuel-cell-powered cars with its redesigned Mirai, which generates electricity to power the car motor without needing recharging but requires a steady supply of hydrogen from an onboard tank.
According to the Department of Energy Alternative Fuels Data Center, there are only a handful of hydrogen filling stations all in California, leading to slow adoption.
Toyota recently announced a partnership with Shell to build more hydrogen stations while simultaneously developing a dedicated EV platform.
Toyota will add two EVs and a plug-in hybrid to its selection in 2021 and will offer electrified versions of various models between now and 2025.
Volkswagen committed to becoming an EV-focused manufacturer following the "dieselgate" scandal, with its Group including Audi, Bentley, Bugatti, Lamborghini, Porsche, and Volkswagen.
The corporation says it will launch 70 pure electric vehicles and 60 hybrids by the end of the decade, some already in production.

VW's most recent addition is the ID.4 small electric SUV aimed at mass market consumers, while Audi already had an E-Tron model in production and recently introduced its electric flagship E-Tron GT built on the same platform as the Porsche Taycan EV.

Bentley CEO says all Bentleys will be plug-ins (hybrid or pure electric) by 2026 and all-electric by the end of the decade, while Lamborghini unveiled the Terzo Millennio concept in 2017 inviting speculation into its electrification plans.
Volvo plans to build only pure electric vehicles by the end of the decade, having been owned by Zhejiang Geely Holding Group (which also owns Lotus) since 2010.
Volvo already has one electric model out, the XC40 Recharge, and Polestar once its performance sub-brand is slated to be all-electric soon.
source_title: Automakers Are Adding Electric Vehicles to Lineups

facts: Adient plc is a global leader in the automotive seating supply industry with leading market positions in the Americas, Europe and China.
Adient operates more than 200 wholly- and majority-owned manufacturing or assembly facilities, with operations in 29 countries.
Adient has more than 70,000 employees operating in its global manufacturing and assembly plants.

Adient designs, manufactures and markets a full range of seating systems and components for passenger cars, commercial vehicles and light trucks, including vans, pick-up trucks and sport/crossover utility vehicles.

Adient's proprietary technologies extend into virtually every area of automotive seating solutions, including complete seating systems, frames, mechanisms, foam, head restraints, armrests and trim covers.

Adient maintains longstanding relationships with premier global OEM customers including BMW, Mercedes-Benz Group, Ford Motor Company, General Motors Company, Honda Motor Company, Hyundai Motor Company, Jaguar Land Rover, Kia Corporation, Mazda Motor Corporation, Mitsubishi Motor Corporation, Nissan Motor Corporation, Renault Group, Stellantis N.V., Suzuki Motor Corporation, Toyota Motor Corporation, Volkswagen Group and Volvo Car Group.

Adient also supplies regional OEMs such as Beijing Automotive Group CO., Ltd., Changan Automobile (Group) Co., Ltd., FAW Group Corporation, Proton Holdings Berhad, Ashok Leyland, Tata Motors Limited and Zhejiang Geely Holding Group Co., Ltd., as well as newer auto manufacturers NIO and Xpeng Motors.
Adient has more than 6 joint venture partnerships with key OEMs, including Guangzhou Automobile Group Co., Ltd., Beijing Automobile Group Co., Ltd. and FAW Group Corporation.
Electric vehicles (“EVs”) continue to be a focus in the global automotive industry driven by product offerings from legacy manufacturers and new entrants.
The rollout of EV platforms varies across regions with factors such as pricing, affordability, government incentives, infrastructure and overall consumer acceptance influencing adoption pace.
While seating systems are not largely impacted by the shift to EVs, key attributes of seat design are evolving as the market pivots toward EVs.

This EV market movement provides Adient with opportunities to provide value-added solutions through its Evolution of Seating Systems Sustainability (“ES3”) and to capture market share through new entrants based on its existing leading market position.
Adient is developing interiors concepts for autonomous driving that address major seating and interior trends expected to drive the automotive industry of the future.
Adient will continue to partner with OEMs and other customers in the development of Advanced Driver Assist Systems (“ADAS”) and Automated Driving Systems (“ADS”) concepts.
Raw materials used by Adient include steel, aluminum, polyurethane chemicals, fabrics, leather, vinyl and polypropylene.
In fiscal year 2021, Adient set a goal of reducing its scope 1 and 2 greenhouse gas emissions 75% by 2030 (with 2019 as the base year).
source_title: Adient plc - Annual Report: 2023 (Form 10-K) | Annual Statements

facts: Constellium was established as an independent entity on January 4, 2011.
The company was formed through the acquisition of Rio Tinto's Alcan Engineered Products (AEP) business, which encompassed aluminum rolled products, extrusions, and value-added services.
The transaction involved Omega Holdco B.V., a Dutch private limited liability company incorporated on May 14, 2010, later renamed Constellium Holdco B.V.
Apollo Global Management secured a 51% ownership stake through its funds.
Rio Tinto retained a 39% minority interest and the French sovereign wealth fund Fonds Stratégique d'Investissement (FSI) held 10%.
Post-acquisition, the company achieved €49 million in annual savings in 2011.
The AEP business was rebranded as Constellium in May 2011.
Constellium inherited legacy operations from aluminum pioneers including Pechiney and Alusuisse.
The company generated €3.55 billion in sales for the year of its formation.
Constellium N.V. completed its initial public offering on May 23, 2013, pricing 22.2 million Class A ordinary shares at $15 each.
The IPO raised approximately $333 million and listed shares on the New York Stock Exchange under ticker "CSTM" and Euronext Paris.
In August 2013, Constellium completed a €23 million modernization of its Neuf-Brisach facility in France for aluminum recycling and production.
By April 2014, the company announced €43 million in investments for two new casthouses at its Issoire plant in France for aerospace applications.
In May 2014, Constellium committed €15 million to expand casting and extrusion lines at its Decin facility in the Czech Republic for automotive hard alloys.
An additional €22.5 million investment was announced in February 2015 to boost fabrication capacity.
In April 2015, Constellium unveiled a $40 million investment at its Van Buren Township facility near Detroit, Michigan.
The Michigan expansion added 210,000 square feet of manufacturing space and created over 200 jobs for aluminum-intensive body structures.
In January 2015, Constellium acquired Wise Alloys, gaining control of aluminum rolling operations.
The company committed up to $750 million through 2022 for upgrades at the Muscle Shoals plant in Alabama.
Revenue reached approximately $7.3 billion in 2024, supported by expanded production and demand for lightweight aluminum solutions.
Adjusted EBITDA for 2024 totaled $550 million.
In 2018, Constellium invested in new homogenizing furnaces and casthouse equipment to boost aluminum processing capabilities.
By 2024, the Muscle Shoals, Alabama plant received a $23 million U.S. Department of Defense grant to rebuild its casting center.
The grant adds up to 136,000 metric tons of annual capacity focused on recycled aluminum for defense and commercial applications.

In January 2019, Constellium acquired full ownership of its U.S. auto body sheet joint venture in Bowling Green, Kentucky, purchasing the remaining 49% stake from UACJ for $100 million plus proportional debt.
The company signed a multi-year contract with Airbus for aerospace aluminum products supporting all programs.
In September 2025, Constellium extended a long-term supply agreement with Embraer for advanced alloys like Airware®.
Constellium leads the £15 million ALIVE project for aluminum battery enclosures in electric vehicles.
The company committed to reduce Scope 1 and 2 greenhouse gas emissions by 25% by 2025 from a 2018 baseline.
Constellium SE is a publicly traded company listed on the NYSE under ticker CSTM, with over 96% institutional ownership as of mid-2025.
Major shareholders include T. Rowe Price Investment Management (9.75%), Fidelity Management & Research Co. LLC (9.41%), BlackRock, FMR LLC, and Bpifrance SA.
Chief Executive Officer Jean-Marc Germain oversees overall strategy and operations.
Senior Vice President and Group General Counsel Stephen Walters serves as Secretary to the Board.
The Board of Directors is chaired by Jean-Christophe Deslarzes and comprises ten directors as of 2025.
Bradley Soultz was appointed as a new non-executive director at the May 15, 2025 Annual General Meeting.
Constellium operates through three primary business units: Aerospace & Transportation (A&T), Packaging & Automotive Rolled Products (P&ARP), and Automotive Structures & Industry (AS&I).
The company leverages rolling, extrusion, and casting processes across 25 production facilities in Europe, North America, and China.
Constellium employs approximately 12,000 employees globally.
The Ravenswood plant in West Virginia employs over 1,100 workers and features advanced equipment for high-strength aluminum sheets.
The Muscle Shoals facility in Alabama has about 1,250 employees and over 100 acres of covered production space with world-class recycling capabilities.
The Singen, Germany facility employs more than 1,500 people and handles hot/cold rolling alongside a large extrusion press for automotive products.
Neuf-Brisach, France has a 450,000 metric ton annual capacity for canstock, food stock, and automotive sheet.
The Issoire, France facility employs 1,600 workers and focuses on aerospace plates using proprietary Airware® technology.
Děčín, Czech Republic operates with 100,000 metric tons per year capacity in casting and extrusion for hard alloys.
The Bowling Green, Kentucky facility produces flat-rolled automotive body sheet.
The White, Georgia facility is dedicated to advanced automotive structural components and crash management systems.
The San Luis Potosí, Mexico facility produces crash systems with integrated quality labs.
A joint venture in Changchun, China produces automotive crash management and structural parts.
Constellium sources aluminum inputs including primary molten metal, slabs, billets, and scrap without engaging in bauxite mining or primary smelting.
The company uses Laser-Induced Breakdown Spectroscopy (LIBS) technology to sort high-purity automotive scrap for recycling.
Constellium maintains three dedicated R&D centers with annual investments reaching approximately €52 million in 2023.
The company fosters over 250 active patent families and around 50 scientific partnerships with universities and labs.
C-TEC (Constellium Technology Center) in Voreppe, France employs about 250 specialists across 20 nationalities focusing on alloy development and decarbonization.
The Plymouth, Michigan facility operates as a research and technology hub in the Detroit automotive corridor for tailored aluminum solutions.
The University Technology Center at Brunel University London hosts around 50 Constellium scientists alongside 20 PhD students and postdoctoral fellows.
Constellium supplies fuselage skins, wing panels, and structural elements for commercial and military aircraft using Airware® aluminum-lithium alloys.
The company holds a leading position in large extrusions for railway rolling stock to enable lighter rail cars.

Mack Trucks, Inc. is an American truck manufacturing company and a former manufacturer of buses and trolley buses.
Founded in 1900 as the Mack Brothers Company, it adopted its present name in 1922.
Since 2000, Mack Trucks has been a subsidiary of Volvo, which purchased Mack and its former parent company Renault Véhicules Industriels.
Headquarters moved to Greensboro, North Carolina in 2009.
Key people include Stephen Roy (President).
Products include heavy and medium-duty trucks.
Manufacturing facilities are located at the Lehigh Valley Operations facility / Macungie Assembly Operations Plant in Macungie, Pennsylvania.
Mack Trucks is the fourth-largest employer in the Lehigh Valley region as of 2024.
Mack vehicles are produced in Lower Macungie, Pennsylvania, and Salem, Virginia.
Powertrain products are produced in its Hagerstown, Maryland plant, which also produces and designs all Mack engines, including Mack Defense engines used in tanks and other military applications.
In 2021, Mack completed an upgrade of its facility with $84 million in funding.
In July 2021, US President Joe Biden visited the Mack Trucks plant in Pennsylvania, where he was shown Mack's fully electric Class 8 truck, the Mack LR Electric.
In July 2021, Mack Trucks said it would hire 400 more employees at its facility in Lower Macungie, adding to a 2,500 overall workforce.
In August 2021, it was reported that Mack had to suspend "production periodically" in July 2021 due to chip shortages.
A 2008 restructuring plan included relocating the head office, product development, and most support/purchasing functions to Greensboro, North Carolina in 2009.
The 2008 restructuring plan also included assembly of all produced Mack highway vehicles in Macungie, Pennsylvania, starting in 2008.
Mack's testing facility in Allentown, Pennsylvania was converted into a "customer demonstration and reception center" in 2010.
The company maintains a remanufacturing center in Middletown, Pennsylvania.
Parts for right-hand-drive vehicles are produced in Brisbane, Australia for worldwide distribution.
Assembly for South America is performed in Macungie alongside North American trucks.
In October 2010, Mack announced that a version of its Terrapro Cabover would run on natural gas using a Cummins Westport engine.
In March 2008, Mack introduced the Titan, a heavy duty model with a 16-liter big-block MP10 engine producing 515, 565, and 605 horsepower.
In 2014, Mack offered three engine series: the 11 L MP 7, 13 L MP8, and 16 L MP10, with power ranging from 325hp to 605hp and torque from 1,200lb⋅ft to 2,060lb⋅ft.
Mack is one of the top producers in the vocational and on-road vehicle market, class 8 through class 13.
On April 8, 2025, Mack announced a brand new class 8 tractor, the Pioneer, in Brooklyn, New York.
Novelis Inc. announced an approximately $2.5 billion greenfield, fully integrated rolling and recycling plant in Bay Minette, Alabama, U.S.
Construction of the Bay Minette facility was planned to commence following the May 11, 2022 announcement.
The new rolling mill will support strong demand for sustainable beverage can and automotive aluminum sheet.
Novelis is considering a number of potential capital investment opportunities over the next five years spanning regions and end markets.
Other previously announced U.S. projects include Oswego hot mill debottlenecking and the construction of an automotive recycling center in Guthrie.
During fiscal year 2022, Novelis experienced constrained automotive shipments impacted by the global semiconductor chip shortage.
Novelis provides innovative aluminum solutions to customers in the aerospace, automotive, beverage can, and specialties industries throughout North America, Europe, Asia, and South America.
Novelis Inc. is a subsidiary of Hindalco Industries Limited, the metals flagship company of the Aditya Birla Group.
Total flat rolled product shipments reached a record 3,858 kilotonnes in fiscal year 2022.
Adjusted EBITDA reached a record $2.0 billion in fiscal year 2022.
President and CEO Steve Fisher stated the company is embarking on its next phase of strategic expansion to support customers for the long term.
Executive Vice President and CFO Devinder Ahuja noted that record operational performance provides a solid footing to begin the next wave of transformational investments and expand capacity.
Novelis ended fiscal year 2022 with strong total liquidity of $2.6 billion as of March 31, 2022.

The company expects first quarter fiscal year 2023 Adjusted EBITDA per ton to return to levels above $500 per ton despite continuing impacts from semiconductor shortages and global supply chain challenges.
Novelis reported record net income from continuing operations of $1.0 billion for the full fiscal year 2022.
Adjusted free cash flow from continuing operations was $649 million in fiscal year 2022, after absorbing more than $400 million of increased working capital pressure from rising aluminum prices.
Net sales reached $17.1 billion for the full fiscal year 2022.
The net leverage ratio improved to 2.2x at the end of fiscal year 2022, compared to 2.9x at the end of fiscal year 2021.

facts: Georgia manufacturing employers submitted 594 H-1B petitions during 2024.
Of these, 581 were approved (97.8%) and 13 were denied (2.2%).
The H-1B visa program allows U.S. employers to temporarily hire foreign workers in specialty occupations requiring specialized knowledge and at least a bachelor’s degree or equivalent.
The program is designed to address skill gaps in the U.S. workforce while ensuring wage standards to protect both U.S. and H-1B workers.
Employers must certify that they will pay H-1B workers wages equal to or greater than those of similarly qualified U.S. employees or the prevailing local wage.
The average approval rate for H-1B petitions across the United States in 2024 stood at 98%, higher than 2023's 97.3%.
Georgia employers across all industries submitted 11,933 petitions during 2024 with a 96.3% approval rate.
The Professional, Scientific, and Technical Services industry led H-1B filings in Georgia with 6,763 petitions, representing 56.7% of all submissions.
The Educational Services industry was the next largest contributor with 1,114 petitions (9.3%), of which 6,512 were approved and 251 were denied.
Gulfstream Aerospace Corporation filed the most manufacturing sector petitions in Georgia during 2024 with 37.
Mohawk Industries, Inc. filed 30 manufacturing sector petitions in Georgia during 2024.
Georgia Pacific, LLC filed 25 manufacturing sector petitions in Georgia during 2024.
The Manufacturing sector in Georgia saw a 16.2% increase in H-1B petitions compared to 2023.
The United States admits around 1 million legal immigrants annually, most through family sponsorship or employment-based visas.
In 2023, about 1.2 million immigrants were granted green cards, returning to pre-pandemic levels.
Temporary visa programs like H-1B for highly skilled workers and H-2A for seasonal agricultural workers play a critical role in filling workforce needs.
A 2024 Pew Research Center survey found 46% of Americans favor maintaining current legal immigration levels.

According to the same survey, 42% believe highly skilled workers should be prioritized for legal immigration, compared to 25% prioritizing labor shortage workers and 19% emphasizing family sponsorship.
Family sponsorship remains the most common path to permanent residency, accounting for 63% of green cards issued in 2023.
Data comes from the H-1B Employer Data Hub provided by USCIS, though manual entry errors may cause discrepancies in employer names, tax IDs, or locations.
