facts: In addition to service industries, the economy of Gordon County is rooted in manufacturing and both heavy and light industry.
Mohawk Industries, a leading manufacturer of flooring, is headquartered in Gordon County.
Shaw Industries has a significant presence in Gordon County.
Beaulieu International Group has a significant presence in Gordon County.
LG Chem has a significant presence in Gordon County.
Kobelco Construction Machinery America - a division of Kobe Steel - has a significant presence in Gordon County.
Gordon County has the most interstate exits out of all of the counties in Georgia, because of its location in the middle of Interstate 75.
Ascend Elements, Inc. is a lithium-ion battery recycler headquartered in Westborough, MA.
The company recovers critical minerals from end-of-life EV batteries and gigafactory scrap.
It converts recovered materials into battery-grade products including black mass, precursor cathode active material (pCAM), and lithium carbonate.
The company's proprietary Hydro-to-Cathode® process combines hydrometallurgy with direct precursor synthesis and is licensed from Worcester Polytechnic Institute (WPI).
The technology underpins a worldwide intellectual property portfolio of 154 granted and pending patents.
Ascend operates an active commercial facility in Covington, GA (Apex 0).
Apex 0 is a 154,000-square-foot plant located approximately 35 miles east of Atlanta.
The Covington facility was originally announced in January 2022 as a $43 million investment.
During the August 2025 facility restart, CEO Linh Austin disclosed that over $120 million had been invested in Apex 0, representing a near-threefold overrun relative to the original budget.
Apex 0 is designed to process approximately 30,000 metric tons of battery feedstock per year into 20,000–30,000 metric tons of black mass.
In August 2025, Apex 0 produced what the company described as the first commercial-scale recycled lithium carbonate (>99% pure) in U.S. history.
The Covington facility experienced fourteen fire-related incidents between March 2023 and February 2025.
Operations at Apex 0 were suspended in February 2025 under pressure from the Covington Fire Department.
Apex 0 operations restarted on August 25, 2025, following safety upgrades and a near-total site management replacement.
Utility-connection upgrades remain necessary for commercially viable lithium carbonate output at Apex 0.
Ascend Elements filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection on April 9, 2026, in the U.S. Bankruptcy Court for the Southern District of Texas.
The company reported $1 billion to $10 billion in assets and $500 million to $1 billion in liabilities at filing.
Ascend is pursuing an expedited 363 sale of substantially all assets.
Apex 1 in Hopkinsville, KY is a 60% complete, construction-paused pCAM plant targeting pCAM for roughly 750,000 EVs per year.
Apex 1 was announced in August 2022 as a Phase 1 $310 million investment with eventual buildout up to $1 billion.
The company operates a 50/50 Polish joint venture with Elemental Strategic Metals in Zawiercie (AE Elemental) that commercially opened September 19, 2024.
AE Elemental operates a 12,000 MT/year battery metals recovery facility.
Ascend has an early-stage wholly-owned Polish lithium carbonate and pCAM extraction project (Apex 2) in the Wałbrzych Special Economic Zone.
The company also operates an R&D facility in Westborough, MA, and a cathode-finishing lab in Novi, MI.
Headcount was approximately 102 at the petition date, with estimated 2024 revenue of approximately $35 million.
Ascend has a November 2025 multi-year offtake agreement with Trafigura Group for 15,000 metric tons of lithium carbonate between 2027 and 2031.
The Trafigura contract is described by Ascend as a "take-or-pay" agreement.
In December 2025, Ascend signed a multi-year supply contract valued at approximately $1 billion with an unnamed leading global automaker.
In June 2023, Ascend signed a pCAM supply contract valued at approximately $1 billion (with an option to expand up to $5 billion) for an unnamed major U.S. company, sourced from Apex 1.
On March 14, 2023, Ascend signed an agreement with Koura (Orbia's fluorinated solutions business) for up to 5,000 metric tons per year of recycled lithium carbonate.
Orbia Ventures led the company's 2021 Series B financing.
Ascend claims more than $2 billion in aggregate future commitments, with each of the three largest contracts structurally dependent on Apex 1 completion.
A non-binding February 2023 basic agreement was signed with Honda.
The company made one-time commercial deliveries to Navitas Systems for an undisclosed DoD project and to Freudenberg e-Power Systems.
Under CEO Mike O'Kronley, the company raised approximately $1 billion of equity over three years (2021–2024).
A $300 million October 2022 Series C financing included $200 million in equity led by Fifth Wall Climate, with SK ecoplant joining as a strategic investor.

A $542 million September 2023 Series D was led by Decarbonization Partners (a BlackRock/Temasek JV), Temasek, and Qatar Investment Authority, with Fifth Wall, BHP Ventures, Tenaska, and Alliance Resource Partners participating.
A $162 million February 2024 round came from Just Climate, Clearvision Ventures, and IRONGREY.
SK ecoplant was Ascend's largest individual equity holder, committing $60.84 million across Series C and a follow-on, and taking a board seat.
On September 9, 2024, SK ecoplant sold its entire stake (~7.7% of shares) to Seoul-based SKS Private Equity Co., Ltd. for approximately $98.23 million.
SK ecoplant separately sold its waste- and water-treatment business to KKR for approximately $1.1–$1.2 billion in August 2025.
Battery Resourcers, LLC was founded in 2015 as a WPI spinout by Dr. Yan Wang (Chief Scientist), Dr. Eric Gratz (CEO), and Dr. Diran Apelian (Board Chair).
Mike O'Kronley was appointed CEO in March 2020 after joining the board in 2017.
The company rebranded as Ascend Elements in January 2022.
O'Kronley departed on or about March 20, 2025.
Linh Austin was appointed President and CEO on March 20, 2025, after joining the board in September 2024.
Eight of eleven senior leaders have joined since 2025.
Adam Titus served as interim CFO beginning January 2026 and transitioned to Chief Restructuring Officer on April 3, 2026.
Ahmed Allouache was appointed permanent CFO on March 21, 2026.
Lithium carbonate prices peaked at approximately $80,000 per metric ton in November 2022 before collapsing to roughly $8,259 per ton by June 2025.
As of 2023, China controlled nearly 85% of global battery cell production capacity by monetary value and over two-thirds of the world's cobalt and lithium processing capacity as of 2022.
Spot battery-grade lithium carbonate roughly doubled in Q1 2026, rising from approximately $13,433 per metric ton in early December 2025 to approximately $26,278 per metric ton by late January 2026.
USTR Section 301 tariffs on Chinese lithium-ion EV batteries rose from 7.5% to 25% effective September 27, 2024.
The One Big Beautiful Bill Act, signed July 4, 2025, terminated Section 30D's $7,500 consumer EV tax credit for vehicles acquired after September 30, 2025.
The act also narrowed Section 45X advanced manufacturing credits with new Foreign Entity of Concern restrictions.
The Section 30D termination triggered a 28% year-over-year collapse in Q1 2026 U.S. new EV sales.
In October 2022, the DOE awarded Ascend two Battery Materials Processing grants totaling approximately $480 million under the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law.
A $316.2 million grant for pCAM infrastructure and a $164 million grant for CAM manufacturing infrastructure were awarded.
The $164 million CAM grant was mutually canceled on February 27, 2025.
The $316.2 million pCAM grant was unilaterally canceled by the DOE on or about October 7, 2025.
Approximately $207 million had been disbursed at termination, leaving approximately $110 million unfunded.
DOE stated the canceled projects missed milestones, were not economically viable, and would not provide a positive return on investment of taxpayer dollars.
The DOE retains an undivided reversionary interest in equipment acquired with grant funds.

facts: Aspen Aerogels, Inc. operates as a technology leader in sustainability and electrification solutions, leveraging aerogel technology for e-mobility and clean energy applications.
The company's PyroThin® products are specifically engineered to address thermal runaway challenges within the electric vehicle ("EV") market.
Aspen Aerogels is demobilizing its previously planned second manufacturing plant in Statesboro, Georgia.
Planned asset sales in Georgia are expected to materially reduce the company's debt over the coming quarters.
Capital expenditures related to the Statesboro plant project totaled $11.3 million in the first half of 2025.
Second quarter 2025 revenue was $78.0 million, with the Thermal Barrier segment contributing $55.2 million, a 13% increase quarter-over-quarter.
First half of 2025 actual revenue totaled $157 million, while the second half of 2025 outlook projects revenue between $140 million and $160 million.
Full year 2025 revenue outlook is set at $297 million to $317 million.
Adjusted EBITDA for the second quarter of 2025 was $9.7 million, with a second half of 2025 outlook of $20 million to $30 million and a full year outlook of $35 million to $45 million.
The company ended the second quarter of 2025 with $167.6 million in cash and equivalents.
CEO Don Young cited regulatory headwinds facing the EV market, particularly in the U.S., as a driver for restructuring the company to operate with minimal capital expenditure requirements.
Aspen Aerogels announced that CFO Ricardo Rodriguez will step down at the end of the third quarter, with Grant Thoele appointed as his internal successor effective immediately upon departure.
Second quarter 2025 net loss was $9.1 million, which included $5.9 million in restructuring and impairment charges.
The company's strategy focuses on partnering with industry leaders to leverage its Aerogel Technology Platform into additional high-value markets.
A conference call to discuss second quarter 2025 results and recent business developments will be held Thursday, August 7, 2025 at 8:30 a.m. EST.

The global active smart glass market is driven by the increasing use of smart glass in luxury and electric vehicles.

On July 14, 2025, Gauzy Ltd. in collaboration with Research Frontiers announced the first customer delivery of General Motors’ Cadillac CELESTIQ, its first fully electric vehicle sedan featuring patented Suspended Particle Device (SPD) smart glass technology supplied by Gauzy.
Car manufacturers are increasingly turning to active smart glass for sunroofs, side windows, rear windows and panoramic sunroofs.
Active smart glass technology helps make passengers more comfortable, reduces heat build-up in the cabin and makes the car more energy efficient, which is especially crucial for electric vehicles.
Automakers are seeking innovative features to differentiate premium and next-generation mobility platforms.

On January 9, 2025, Gauzy Ltd. unveiled its latest suspended particle device (SPD) Smart Glass technology at CES 2025, created to accommodate demand from the automotive, aeronautics, and architecture industries.

On January 7, 2025, Gentex Corporation announced that it is set to debut a host of “next-gen” products, including display-integrated dimmable sun visors, digital rearview mirrors with OLED displays, mirror-integrated driver and in-cabin monitoring systems, and enhancements to HomeLink.

Demand for active smart glass in Germany is being driven by the automotive industry as premium vehicle manufacturers integrate switchable glass technology into panoramic roofs and glazing systems for luxury vehicles to improve comfort and energy management for passengers.
The proliferation of intelligent transportation systems and premium automotive applications is likely to generate strong growth possibilities for active smart glass.

facts: The automotive parts manufacturing industry in Alabama thrives as a crucial segment of the broader automotive ecosystem.
Companies produce a wide range of vital components, from powertrain parts to interior elements, catering to major automotive manufacturers.
Many firms are adopting more sustainable practices as technology advances.
There is a growing emphasis on lightweight materials and electric vehicle components, reflecting a broader shift towards fuel efficiency and emissions reduction.
The sector contributes significantly to the state's economy by creating jobs and positions Alabama as a key player in the automotive supply chain.
The listed companies represent a mix of sizes and specialties, ranging from small operations to large corporations.
These firms were founded between 1954 and 2017 and are mostly privately owned.
Headquarters are located throughout various Alabama cities such as Montgomery, Auburn, and Opelika.
Hyundai MOBIS Alabama, LLC operates from a nearly 83-acre facility in Montgomery, Alabama, established in 2002.
Hyundai MOBIS is the largest Tier 1 supplier to Hyundai Motor Manufacturing Alabama and Kia Motor Manufacturing Georgia.
Hyundai MOBIS manufactures complete cockpit modules, front and rear chassis modules, bumper assemblies, and instrument panels.
Hyundai MOBIS manages a Redistribution Center supplying genuine Hyundai and Kia service parts across North America.
Seohan Auto USA Corporation is located in Auburn, Alabama, established in 2007, and specializes in automotive parts, wind power components, and forging services.
Seohan Auto USA focuses on high-quality driveline and powertrain parts, including solenoid valves, pumps, and actuators.
Alto Products Corp is based in Atmore, Alabama, founded in 1954, and specializes in wet friction materials and clutches.
Alto has produced over a billion friction clutches and serves automotive, heavy-duty, marine, and motorcycle sectors.
Bocar US, Inc. is an automotive supplier in Tanner, Alabama, established in 2017, specializing in aluminum and plastic components.
Bocar produces complex assemblies through high-pressure die casting and injection molding processes.
Moriroku Technology North America - Rainsville Plant was established in 2000 in Rainsville, Alabama, producing interior and exterior components primarily for Honda.
REGITAR USA, Inc. has been based in Montgomery, Alabama, since 1987, specializing in ignition and charging system parts, safety products, and power tools.
TSE Brakes is a Marmon/Berkshire Hathaway Company based in Cullman, Alabama, operational since 1966, manufacturing brake chambers and slack adjusters for OEMs and the aftermarket.
Hanwha Advanced Materials America, LLC was founded in 2007 in Opelika, Alabama, producing lightweight composite materials like StrongLite and BuffLite for automotive fuel efficiency.
Hanwha Advanced Materials produces bumper beams, seat structures, underbody shields, and interior trim panels, and acquired the GMT business unit from Azdel.

facts: Rivian plans to begin building a giant electric vehicle factory in Georgia soon.
The proposed plant site is located about 300 yards from Clint Powell’s home in Social Circle.
Powell owns a two-acre lot and has lived in the area for 17 years.
Residents are exploring ways to stop or limit the project, including throwing up possible zoning roadblocks.
Local and state authorities negotiated in secret with Rivian for months last year while keeping residents in the dark.
The site is situated in a largely rural area approximately 45 miles east of Atlanta.
The development includes a 2,500-acre combined-use industrial park.
$75 million is designated for road improvements related to the project.
A local opposition group called 'Rivian Plant Opposed Group Local In Facebook' has formed.
Eric Allsman is listed as a contact for the opposition group.
Rivian originally announced the project back in December 2021.
Residents are demanding answers on how many jobs will be created and what the overall impact will be.
The Local Economic Development Department in Atlanta is a contact point for the project.
The project is in the early stages of development.
source_title: Neighbors in Social Circle and Rutledge left out of the discussion on Rivian Georgia EV plant
